tweets.title: I heard someone argue that air-to-air missiles are ordered in tranches because they have a finite service life. That’s correct but that is only part of the story and from very user seller point of view. Take the Indian Air Force’s order for roughly 240 Astra missiles. Yes, a missile has a shelf life, and yes, if it is not kept in a temperature-controlled environment its life can reduce further. All of that is fair. But the real issue is: if you place missile orders with a clear long-term roadmap, the manufacturing ecosystem can plan properly. It knows how much to produce, when to deliver, and where to invest in infrastructure. It can build a little extra capacity to handle crisis situations, so you are not forced to look outside for emergency purchases when demand suddenly spikes. That is why a 20-year roadmap for missiles and manufacturing makes far more sense than simply ordering 240 today and then waiting to see when all the MKI are ready to carry them, or when the Mk1A fleet finally arrives. That should not be just procurement. It should be full lifecycle planning with proper phase out replacement etc built it. Instead we are arguing we will order it when MKI are modified or MK1A are delivered. This very thought process has placed IAF where it stands today.
tweets.content:

tweets.title: 🚨 Red Fort Blast: Delhi Court Orders Dignified Last Rites for Victims After Months of Investigation 📍Delhi court has permitted the NIA to cremate and dispose of the remains of 11 victims killed in the November 2025 Red Fort terror blast, more than seven months after the attack. 📍The court also allowed the disposal of the remains of suicide bomber Dr. Umer Un Nabi, who died after detonating the explosive laden vehicle. 📍The NIA informed the court that all required forensic examinations had been completed. 📍The court directed that all remains be disposed of with full dignity while respecting the religious beliefs of the victims. tweets.content:
🚨 Red Fort Blast: Delhi Court Orders Dignified Last Rites for Victims After Months of Investigation

📍Delhi court has permitted the NIA to cremate and dispose of the remains of 11 victims killed in the November 2025 Red Fort terror blast, more than seven months after the attack.

📍The court also allowed the disposal of the remains of suicide bomber Dr. Umer Un Nabi, who died after detonating the explosive laden vehicle.

📍The NIA informed the court that all required forensic examinations had been completed.

📍The court directed that all remains be disposed of with full dignity while respecting the religious beliefs of the victims. It also sought a compliance report from the NIA.

📍The NIA had earlier filed a 7,500 page chargesheet in the Red Fort terror blast case on May 14 as its investigation continues.

Comprising 10 large signs/letters made of Gypsum (each sign being appx. 36 cm tall) and the entire inscription stretching nearly 3 metres in length.

The site, located on the arid island of Khadir in Gujarat, was extensively excavated by the @ASIGoI over 13 field seasons from 1989 to 2005, unveiling indigenous urban sophistication in ancient Bharat.

From advanced water engineering to public inscriptions, Dholavira continues to reveal the remarkable capabilities of the Indus-Saraswati people, a legacy deeply rooted in the cultural and civilisational heritage of Bharat.
